original_abstract: Single-pixel imaging offers a powerful framework for capturing scenes without a direct line of sight to the object; however, maintaining high image fidelity remains a significant challenge, particularly in the presence of ambient noise. In this work, we present a single-pixel imaging method that achieves high-quality reconstruction by directly acquiring the scene's Fourier spectrum. By employing phase-shifted sinusoidal structured illumination, we can retrieve the complex spectrum and reconstruct the target image via an inverse Fourier transform. This approach removes the requirement for a direct view of the scene, allowing for flexible detector placement limited only by the ability to collect reflected light. Furthermore, the technique leverages a compressive sampling framework, enabling accurate image reconstruction from sub-Nyquist measurements. We experimentally demonstrate the robustness of this method by recovering clear images using a non-line-of-sight detector, even under conditions of substantial environmental illumination noise.
humanized_abstract: Single-pixel imaging enables us to see the world around us without looking directly at the object of interest. However, capturing the resulting images in high resolution while accounting for ambient background light remains a significant challenge. Here, a new approach for single-pixel imaging to record high-resolution images in the presence of background light is demonstrated. Instead of optically encoding the light scattered from the scene of interest, the Fourier spectrum of the scene is directly recorded and the target image is derived from the complex spectrum using an inverse Fourier transform by illuminating the scene with phase-shifted sinusoidal patterns using structured illumination. The optical encoding required to enable non-line-of-sight imaging is reduced to only requiring the scattered light to be detected, leaving much flexibility in the placement of the detector. In addition, the technique can benefit from compressive sensing. The effectiveness of the approach is demonstrated by demonstrating non-line-of-sight imaging with a detector resolution limited by the reflectance of light and achieving the reconstruction of high-resolution images from the single-pixel data even in the presence of a high level of ambient noise.

The optical encoding required to enable non-line-of-sight imaging is reduced to only requiring the scattered light to be detected, leaving much flexibility in the placement of the detector.
